480:
Iran just put a price tag on who gets to move oil through the Strait of Hormuz, and the currency is yuan. That sounds far away, but it's the kind of move that eventually shows up in our electric bill, fare increases, and the price of goods at palengke.

WHAT IRAN JUST DID
After US–Israeli strikes that killed Iran's Supreme Leader and triggered a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran is now signaling it may allow tankers to pass—but only if they trade in Chinese yuan instead of US dollars. This chokepoint normally carries around 20 million barrels of oil a day, plus a big chunk of the world's LNG supply.

THE "YUAN CONDITION" IN SIMPLE TERMS
Think of the Strait of Hormuz as a narrow valve the world needs to keep energy flowing. Iran is basically saying: if your oil deal is in dollars, good luck; if it's in yuan, we can talk. Behind the scenes, reports say a "shadow fleet" has already been moving millions of barrels of Iranian oil in yuan-denominated deals, outside of Western sanctions.

PETRODOLLAR VS PETRO-YUAN
For decades, most countries had to hold US dollars to buy oil, which kept demand for the dollar high and borrowing costs low for America. If more barrels start moving only when paid in yuan, countries that rely on Gulf oil will quietly build yuan reserves instead of dollar reserves. Some analysts are now worried about a world where there are two prices for oil: cheaper yuan barrels for China's allies, and higher dollar barrels for everyone else.

CHINA'S QUIET ADVANTAGE
China says it is "concerned" about disruption, but it is also negotiating to keep its own tankers and oil contracts flowing. If Beijing locks in reliable yuan-based access while others scramble, it gets cheaper energy and strengthens its currency's role in global trade. That's how a country moves from just buying oil to quietly becoming a kind of security guarantor for the region.

PANIC, HARD ASSETS, AND BITCOIN
On the financial side, people like Robert Kiyosaki have amplified this story, warning that a "yuan oil" world would accelerate a shift away from the dollar. Gold and other so-called safe havens have been jumping as investors look for shelter from a possible dollar shock. Volatility feels like chaos in the moment, but it's also where big re-pricing happens, including for assets like crypto.

FILIPINO LENS: BAKIT IMPORTANTE SA ATIN?
When a chokepoint like Hormuz is weaponized, countries like the Philippines don't get to negotiate. We import almost all our oil and fuel. Every extra dollar on oil filters into diesel, electricity, transport, and eventually the cost of pamasahe and pagkain. The uncomfortable question for us is simple: if the world really splits into a dollar side and a yuan side for energy, which side are we on—and who decided that for us?

SOURCES:
1. CNN: Iran May Allow Tankers Through Hormuz Only in Chinese Currency
2. Daily Sabah / Anadolu Agency: Iran may permit oil tankers to pass Hormuz if trade uses yuan
3. Türkiye Today: Iran may allow Hormuz oil shipments if traded in yuan
4. Middle East Eye live blog: Iran may allow oil tankers through Strait of Hormuz trading in Chinese yuan
5. NDTV: Iran May Allow Oil Tankers Through Strait Of Hormuz, But There's A China Catch
6. CNN coverage on Iranian oil exports and Hormuz traffic
7. Reuters: Analysts hike oil price outlooks as Iran conflict enters third week
8. Reuters: Iranian oil flows through Strait of Hormuz even as Gulf neighbors' exports shut
9. Reuters video: Global oil and gas shipping costs surge as Iran closes Strait of Hormuz
10. Capital.com: Crude Oil Price Forecast — Strait of Hormuz Closure
11. NBC News: Oil prices volatile on conflicting reports about Strait of Hormuz
12–22. Various Facebook and Reddit sources on yuan-for-passage implications
481:
Sara Duterte filed a 15-page "Answer Ad Cautelam" to the impeachment complaint. This is an important document. Let me walk through what she said, what she didn't say, and what the April 8 trial date actually means.

HOW THE "ANSWER" DODGES THE CASE
The phrase "ad cautelam" means "as a precaution"—she's filing this response without conceding that the Senate has jurisdiction over her impeachment. This is a legal hedge. By using this framing, she preserves her right to argue later that the whole proceeding is invalid, even while responding to the substance.

THE "DOUBLE STANDARD" CARD
Sara's answer argues that the House resolution was politically motivated and that the impeachment was rushed without proper procedure. She invokes what her legal team calls a double standard—claiming she's being treated differently from other officials who faced similar accusations.

FOLLOW THE MONEY
The core financial question is about the P125 million in confidential funds allocated to her office. Her answer doesn't deny that the funds existed or that they were spent. Her defense centers on the claim that the expenditures were lawful and that confidential fund procedures were followed.

"MARY GRACE PIATTOS" AND "KOKOY VILLAMIN"
Two names appear in the testimony record that have become shorthand for the alleged misuse. Sara's answer addresses the testimony of witnesses who claimed to have received cash from these intermediaries, but disputes the characterization of those transactions.

RAMIL MADRIAGA AND THE BAGS OF CASH
Ramil Madriaga, a former aide, gave testimony about cash distributions allegedly traced back to Sara's confidential funds. Her answer challenges his credibility and the chain of custody of the alleged evidence.

THE ASSASSINATION LINE
Perhaps the most widely reported part of her earlier statements was the allegation that she had planned for the assassination of President Marcos, First Lady Liza Araneta-Marcos, and House Speaker Martin Romualdez if she were killed. Her legal filing walks back or contextualizes this statement.

THE ONE-YEAR BAR AND THE TECHNICAL ESCAPE
Under the Constitution, an official can only be impeached once per year. Sara's legal team argues that the timeline of the House proceedings may have violated this bar, which could provide grounds for dismissal on procedural grounds.

WHO'S STILL WITH HER
Despite the impeachment, Sara still has political allies, particularly in Mindanao and among Duterte loyalists. Her legal team includes prominent lawyers who have handled high-profile political cases.

THE "FISHING EXPEDITION" LINE
Sara's answer repeatedly uses the phrase "fishing expedition" to describe the Senate proceedings—arguing that prosecutors are looking for crimes to charge her with rather than investigating a specific, well-defined accusation.

WHAT APRIL 8 IS REALLY ABOUT
April 8 is set as a preliminary conference date, not the full trial. The real question is whether the Senate impeachment court proceeds to full trial, what evidence gets admitted, and whether Sara shows up at all.

ALL THE THINGS SHE DIDN'T DENY
Here is where the document becomes most revealing. Her answer does not directly deny: that P125 million in confidential funds was disbursed; that cash was given to individuals identified in testimony; that intermediaries named in testimony existed and worked with her office. She challenges the characterization and legality of those acts—but not the basic facts.

SOURCES (14 cited in original article)
482:
It took me several days to research this article—if you scroll down to the source list, you'll understand why. Listening to Lacson and Marcoleta argue about the Kalayaan Group of Islands, I realized something unsettling: both of them were wrong in important ways—but for different reasons. So this blog isn't a final verdict or a patriotic speech. It's my attempt, as one Filipino reader, to actually trace what the documents say.

We need to say this upfront, kasi mahalaga: the Kalayaan Island Group (KIG) is ours to defend. Not because Tomas Cloma "discovered" empty islands in 1956, but because for decades the Philippine state has administered, garrisoned, and built community in those islands.

This piece is a byproduct of research, caffeine, and curiosity. I'm not a historian, just a student of history trying to trace where Kalayaan really came from based on primary and secondary documents. If I missed something or read a source poorly, I'm happy to be corrected.

As I went deeper into the archives and rewatched the hearings, one thing kept bothering me: the origin story we keep telling is so weak, it makes the real legal grounds easier to attack. The danger is that the fake story is so weak, it makes the real legal grounds easier to attack.

If you stay with me, we'll walk through three things: how the discovery myth grew, what the documents actually show, and why Marcoleta—despite being right about one narrow historical point—is using that truth in a way that weakens our position.

The latest proof that our own narrative is cracking came in a February 2026 hearing, when the Senate turned into something like a history classroom.

On one side, Panfilo Lacson was defending the familiar story many Filipinos grew up with: Tomas Cloma discovered the Spratlys because they were res nullius—the thing belongs to no one. On the other side, Rodante Marcoleta was calling that story a distortion. He argued that by 1956, the Spratlys were already discovered, mapped, claimed, and occupied by Vietnam and others. He kept returning to one idea: our modern claim stands on effective control and state administration—not Cloma.

If you follow Philippine politics, it's jarring to hear that from someone like Marcoleta. I've criticized him sharply in other posts. But on this specific point, the historical record largely supports what he said.

WHY THE DISCOVERY STORY NEVER REALLY WORKED

The discovery story sounds simple. You imagine an empty sea, a brave Filipino pioneer, a flag on an island. But under international law, that story was never legally sufficient. According to my research, by the early 20th century—especially after cases like Island of Palmas—international law had already moved past the idea that simply finding a place gives you permanent ownership of it. Under modern doctrine, discovery only gives what Max Huber in the Island of Palmas case called an inchoate title—a seed of a claim that needs to be developed through actual occupation and administration. Planting a flag or writing a note to "the whole world" is not enough; you need administration, governance, and a continuous state presence. Huber also rejected the idea that islands belong to whoever is closest, so "malapit sa Palawan" by itself doesn't give the Philippines title.

That's already a problem for the way we talk about Cloma. He was a private citizen, not a state. Even if he had stumbled on something new, discovery alone would not establish perfect title, and he was not representing the Philippine government.

The 2016 South China Sea arbitral award doesn't rescue the myth either. The tribunal was clear that it had no jurisdiction to rule on territorial sovereignty; it dealt with maritime zones under UNCLOS, not who owns what island. When it mentions Cloma at all, it's as a businessman trying to devise a commercial scheme who never acquired sovereignty.

So even before we open the archives, the legal scaffolding of the "discovery and donation" story looks fragile.

THE LONG HISTORY BEFORE CLOMA EVEN ARRIVED

Once you look at the timeline, the idea that the Spratlys were "undiscovered" in 1956 collapses fast. Long before Cloma was born, regional powers already treated these waters as part of their administrative and strategic geography.

Vietnamese chronicles under the Nguyen dynasty describe offshore administration, with Emperor Gia Long establishing administrative control over offshore islands in the early 1800s. By 1833, Emperor Minh Mang had officials planting trees on the islands for navigation safety. In 1837, Jean-Louis Taberd published a Note on the Geography of Cochinchina naming and mapping Paracel-area islands.

Those records suggest that regional states weren't guessing where these features were; they were administering them. The very word "Spratly" comes from British activity. In 1843, Captain Richard Spratly recorded features while passing through. By 1877 and 1889, British authorities in Labuan were formally granting guano leases over specific features.

France then moved from maps to formal annexation. In 1930 it claimed sovereignty over the Spratly Island and neighboring features. The French Navy built infrastructure, including a weather station on Itu Aba in 1938. Japan did not treat the area as empty either. In 1939 it invaded the Spratlys, drove out the French garrison, and administered the islands as part of its wartime empire.

When Japan surrendered in 1945, the Allies ordered Japanese forces in the area to surrender to the Republic of China (ROC). France did not simply walk away in silence after the war. In October 1946, it sent the battleship Chevreuil to re-establish a presence. At the same time, the newly formed People's Republic of China (PRC) adopted the nine-dash line.

The 1951 San Francisco Peace Treaty added legal fog: Article 2(f) made Japan renounce the Spratlys without naming a beneficiary. That omission created room for confusion. It did not erase Vietnamese records, British leases, French annexation, or Japanese administration. By the time Cloma's ship left Manila, the sea was already crowded with flags, treaties, and bases.

CLOMA'S GAMBIT AND THE WORLD'S ANSWER

Tomas Cloma was not a cartoon villain. He was a Boholano lawyer, a fishing magnate, and founder of PMI Colleges. He wanted to expand his fishing operations, build a cannery, and tap guano deposits.

On March 1, 1956, he hosted a farewell dinner in Manila. On March 15, the training ship PMI-IV, commanded by his brother Filemon and crewed by forty men, reached the area. Four days later, Cloma issued his "Notice to the Whole World", declaring absolute ownership over the islands he called "Freedomland."

By July 6, Cloma had gone further, declaring a separate government for Freedomland with its capital at Flat Island. If the area had genuinely been res nullius, this might have become a strange footnote in maritime history. Instead, the reaction was immediate and hostile. Taiwan's ROC government summoned the Philippine ambassador to demand explanations, then in June 1956 dispatched a naval task force to re-garrison Itu Aba (Taiping Island). When Cloma stole an ROC flag from Itu Aba, he ended up having to apologize and return it. The PRC's foreign ministry issued a statement on May 29, 1956, reaffirming China's "indisputable" sovereignty. France sent a formal note on June 7. South Vietnam, on June 1, 1956, issued its own communique reasserting historical sovereignty.

The speed, anger, and military moves tell you everything you need to know. The world did not see an empty sea that a Filipino adventurer had just discovered. They saw a contested archipelago where someone was trying to exploit a legal opening.

HOW THE PHILIPPINE STATE QUIETLY REWROTE THE STORY

Inside Malacañang, Cloma's stunt was not viewed as a patriotic masterstroke. Ramon Magsaysay's administration initially saw it as a potential trigger for conflict with Taiwan and Vietnam. Military and foreign policy advisers opposed adopting his claim. Carlos P. Garcia, as Foreign Secretary, had to face furious embassies and formally state that Cloma was acting as a private citizen.

To protect Philippine interests without endorsing the res nullius claim, Garcia floated a trusteeship concept. That stance is crucial: it shows the state did not, at that time, treat Cloma's 'discovery' as a legally sound foundation.

Over the next decade, that tone changed. By 1968, the Armed Forces of the Philippines were already moving to occupy several unoccupied features. Then Marcos did what Marcos usually did: centralized and coerced. In 1974, his government had Cloma arrested. While in detention, Cloma was pressured into signing a Deed of Assignment and Waiver of Rights, surrendering Freedomland to the Philippine government for one peso.

Four years later, on June 11, 1978, Marcos issued Presidential Decree 1596 (PD 1596). PD 1596 did not lean on a romantic story about discovery. Its preamble justified Philippine sovereignty over the newly defined Kalayaan Island Group by citing security, economic interests, and proximity. The decree also drew coordinates that effectively excluded Itu Aba from Philippine claims—a significant concession to Taiwan. By doing that, it built a domestic legal object—Kalayaan Island Group—distinct from the broader "Spratlys" argument. It created a municipality of Kalayaan under Palawan.

In practice, our claim today is expressed through Pag-asa Island—the main inhabited island of Kalayaan. None of that leans on a romantic story about discovery. All of it leans on presence, administration, and legal acts. Kalayaan is ours to defend because we are actually there.

THE ONE THING MARCOLETA GOT RIGHT—AND WHAT HE'S DOING WITH IT

If you stack the documents, one narrow conclusion is hard to escape: Cloma did not "discover" an empty Kalayaan Island Group in 1956. The islands were not res nullius. On that specific historical and legal point, what Marcoleta said in the hearing—that treating Cloma's voyage as a "discovery" of an empty sea is inaccurate—is correct. But in other venues, Marcoleta has floated the idea of simply "giving up" the Kalayaan Island Group because the Cloma discovery myth doesn't hold up. That is a very different argument.

His line: "Ano ba talaga ang pagpapatayan natin? We will offer our lives to defend based on a fraudulent story?" Maritime law experts like Antonio Carpio and Jay Batongbacal have already dismantled that line, stressing that our claim rests on effective control—not Cloma's voyage.

He has also tried to reduce the work of people like Justice Carpio, Commodore Jay Tarriela, and Batongbacal to "satsat" (empty talk). Every time you call serious legal and strategic analysis "satsat," you are telling ordinary Filipinos to ignore the people defending our maritime rights. That weakens us from the inside.

Then there's the origin of some of his talking points. After a meeting with Chinese Ambassador Huang Xilian, Marcoleta went to the floor to relay Beijing's preferred framing on KIG. Pro-China outlets like Asian Century Journal, already flagged by security analysts for repeating Chinese narratives, have amplified his talking points.

So yes, on Cloma's "discovery," he stumbled into the right side of the historical record. But he's not using that truth to harden our legal footing on effective control. He's using it while pushing other lines that soften the public for giving up.

THE REAL RECEIPTS: WHY KALAYAAN IS LEGALLY OURS

If we want a real origin story, we don't need to start in 1956. We can look at the 1734 Murillo Velarde map, often called the "Mother of all Philippine Maps." Drawn in Manila by Father Pedro Murillo Velarde with Filipino artisans, it already shows the Spratly-area features as part of the Philippine archipelagic frame.

Then there's the 1900 Treaty of Washington, where Spain explicitly sold the Philippines to the United States, including islands that had been part of the Spanish colonial domain. In international law, there's also uti possidetis juris—when a colony becomes an independent state, the old colonial boundaries become the new state's international boundaries. If those colonial-era treaties and maps treated far-flung shoals and reefs as part of the Philippines, the successor state inherits that claim.

Fast forward to UNCLOS and the 2016 arbitral award. UNCLOS runs on a simple but powerful idea: land dominates the sea. Because we have a solid chain of colonial-era inclusion, post-colonial state succession, and decades of actual effective control—garrisoning, civilian settlement on Pag-asa, municipal governance—we have the receipts. When you stack up the 1734 map, the 1900 Treaty of Washington, colonial succession rules, continuous effective control through PD 1596 and military presence, and UNCLOS maritime rights, the claim stands without Cloma.

WHY WE NEED TO LET THE MYTH GO ANYWAY

We're in a strange place as a country. We keep defending a discovery myth the documents don't support, while our actual legal strength—effective control, state administration, colonial succession—sits underexplained. It makes us easier to embarrass in international debates, because rival states can point to French annexation notes, Vietnamese imperial decrees, and British guano leases and make Cloma look naive.

We can say: Cloma didn't discover Kalayaan; the sea was never empty; but we have been there for decades, we administer it, we have people living on Pag-asa, and we have colonial-era receipts that predate Cloma by centuries. Letting go of the myth is not surrendering to China or to any politician. It's tightening our own argument.

Kalayaan is ours to defend, just not for the reasons we were taught. And Marcoleta? He may have stumbled onto one argument that actually holds up. But I don't think he's using it to strengthen our position.

SOURCES: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Uo3R0BEOoKBaOfau4m2S4FstC4qgW7dRwwWfK5AQikI/edit?usp=sharing
483:
[An MCT Commentary] Some of you asked why I reacted the way I did when I reposted the news about Toni Yulo-Loyzaga being brought back. Fair question. Let me answer it. But first, the usual reminder: this is just me, thinking out loud. This is my personal take, based on the data I track. I am not trying to tell you what to think. I genuinely don't recommend basing your judgments on what I write. What I do hope is that something here makes you curious enough to go look it up yourself. That's actually the whole point of MCT. If I can get you to pause and read more, I'm happy. Okay. Now to your question.

WHAT HAPPENED
Malacañang reappointed Maria Antonia "Toni" Yulo-Loyzaga as Special Envoy for Disaster Risk Reduction and Management. She was previously DENR Secretary under Marcos Jr. and left in 2025 when he reshuffled his Cabinet. Now she's back, this time with a different title but in a space that sits right at the center of climate negotiations, disaster funding, and international resilience deals. On paper, sure. She has a background in climate and disaster work. Manila Observatory, National Resilience Council, those are real credentials. I get why the Palace framed it the way they did. But I track people. And when I track her, here is what the data shows me.

BEFORE I GO FURTHER
I want to say this clearly: I am not saying she is guilty of any crime. I am not a court. I am not a prosecutor. When I look at her record, the pattern I see raises serious questions about whether this appointment makes sense for a role that demands "unimpeachable" public trust. That pattern is what I want to walk you through.

PALAWAN: THE RANCH, THE RESERVE, AND THE QUESTION NOBODY ANSWERED
In 1975, Proclamation 1387 created the Busuanga Pasture Reserve in Busuanga and Coron, Palawan. Around 40,000 hectares of state-owned grazing land, roughly ten times the size of Manila. Public land. Government property. Around 1976, the Yulo family, through Yulo King Ranch or YKR, secured control over that same area through government pasture arrangements. After 1986, that tract became the subject of sequestration proceedings, ill-gotten wealth questions, and was tagged in some quarters as the largest agrarian anomaly in the country. Fast forward to today: the Department of Agrarian Reform says parts of the YKR area are covered by CARP petitions and agrarian reform distribution processes. Their position is that this is public pasture land under a government agreement, not a clean, uncontested private estate. A farmers' group called Katipunan ng mga Magbubukid sa Busuanga filed land-grabbing and ethics complaints naming Yulo-Loyzaga in connection with the ranch. One case was brought to the Ombudsman in 2023 alleging conflict of interest and ethical violations. In Congress, the Makabayan bloc and Representative France Castro filed resolutions calling for a probe into the conflict-of-interest risk of having a DENR Secretary whose family is historically tied to a disputed reserve that DENR itself oversees. Senator Raffy Tulfo filed a similar resolution. Now here is her side, and I want to be fair about this: she has denied all of it, clearly and repeatedly. She says the Busuanga Pasture Reserve has always been government-owned, that her family never owned it, and that the complaints are politically motivated. The questions were filed, the complaints were raised, Congress wanted a probe, and none of it was ever fully resolved. Conflict of interest goes beyond titles and ownership. Trust is the real issue. Can the public reasonably trust that her decisions were made without fear or favor? That question was never answered. And now she has a new post.

CHOCOLATE HILLS: THE PAPERWORK WAS THERE, THE PROTECTION WASN'T
The Chocolate Hills in Bohol are a National Geological Monument, protected under Proclamation 1037. Not a gray area. A clearly protected site. Captain's Peak Garden and Resort built inside the vicinity of the Chocolate Hills. Some of the hills were physically cut to fit the resort's design. The whole thing went viral in March 2024 and people were furious. Here is where I want to be precise, because the full picture is more complicated than expected. The records show that DENR actually issued a Temporary Closure Order against the resort on 6 September. They followed that with a Notice of Violation on 22 January 2024 for operating without an Environmental compliance. So the paperwork existed. But the resort was still operating, or had resumed operating, by the time the videos hit social media. That is the problem. Yulo-Loyzaga herself went on site and admitted that some hills were damaged. Officials tried to explain how local permits were issued by pointing to land classification and private property arguments. The orders existed. But the orders did not stop the damage. For a DENR Secretary, the job is not just to file paperwork. It is to make sure the protection actually works. That did not happen here.

MASUNGI: WHO DOES THE LAW PROTECT?
Masungi Georeserve in Baras, Rizal is one of the better-known conservation success stories in recent years. It was built on a 2017 MOA that former DENR Secretary Gina Lopez signed, covering around 2,700 hectares. Under Yulo-Loyzaga, DENR formally terminated the older 2002 joint venture contract that underlies the Masungi arrangement. In Senate hearings, she held the line: Masungi is not a special case, everyone has to comply with the law. From a purely legal standpoint, that argument is not crazy. Irregular contracts are irregular contracts, even when the project they support is doing good work. But here is what Masungi's supporters say, and this includes the Masungi Georeserve Foundation and many conservation groups: that while DENR was pressing hard on Masungi's legal technicalities, quarrying interests and other actors were not held to the same standard. I'll be honest: I don't know who is completely right here. This is a genuine legal and policy dispute. The optics, though, sent a clear message to conservation groups. There is a big difference between enforcing the law consistently across the board and enforcing it selectively.

MINING AND THE DEVELOPMENT TILT
When she was first appointed in 2022, the framing from Malacañang was about harnessing mineral resources. She publicly supported small-scale mining with LGU-level monitoring. A wide range of environmental groups, NGOs, and people's organizations have consistently criticized the direction her DENR took. Groups have called on her successor to revoke reclamation projects, seabed quarrying permits, and other approvals made under her watch. She was not pro-mining at all costs. But a broad cross-section of civil society experienced her DENR as development-tilted.

TRAVEL, PERFORMANCE, AND A RETURN WITHOUT CLOSURE
Her foreign travel and participation in international climate and disaster forums became a sustained criticism. It did not always translate well even in those international spaces. At COP28, NGOs specifically complained that the Philippine pavilion under her leadership sidelined vulnerable communities and centered corporate and government voices. That is not a small critique. COP is precisely where countries are supposed to show up for the people most at risk from climate change. By 2025, Marcos replaced her with Raphael Lotilla in a Cabinet reset. Political reporting framed her exit as quiet, professional. She left with Palawan unresolved, Chocolate Hills still a fresh wound, Masungi still contested, and COP28's NGO critique unanswered. And now she is back.

SO WHY DID I REACT THAT WAY
Disaster Risk Reduction is not a ceremonial post. It sits at the intersection of climate finance, land policy, and international credibility. A Special Envoy in this space talks to donors, lenders, and international bodies. They shape where the money goes and what the Philippines commits to. When I track a public official, I look for patterns. Here is what I found: - Ongoing allegations and investigations about conflict of interest around the YKR–Busuanga Pasture... - A DENR tenure where the paperwork existed but the protection sometimes didn't, where Gina Lopez's conservation legacy was partially reversed... - A COP28 appearance where NGOs said vulnerable communities were displaced in favor of corporate space... That is the pattern. That is why I reacted. But let me be crystal about what this pattern really points to. As I mentioned in my post yesterday, this country is not running out of talent. There are so many excellent names in the scientific and resilience spaces, experts who do the quiet, unglamorous work. Choosing to recycle a controversial figure instead of tapping fresh, unburdened talent is a deliberate political choice. My criticism of this appointment is similar to my criticism of the reappointment of Christina Frasco. It is less about the appointee, and more about the machinery doing the appointing. When the administration makes a move like this, it tells us that political accommodation is still being prioritized over performance accountability. My worry? This country loses lives every single year to typhoons, floods, and landslides. The people who represent us in disaster and climate spaces should walk in with their records clean, their conflicts resolved, and their credibility intact. If that is the standard, this appointment does not meet it. That is my take, and it is only my take, based on publicly reported facts, official statements, and my own reading of the patterns. Go check the facts yourself. Tell me where I'm wrong. I mean that.
